content.text: Type inference is TypeScript's ability to determine a variable's type from context — without a manual annotation. The compiler analyses the right-hand side of an assignment, a function body, or a call site, and derives the most specific type it can. You only need to annotate where inference can't do the job.

attrs.alt: TypeScript type inference: the compiler reads source expressions and determines the type for each declaration automatically

content.text: When you initialise a variable, TypeScript infers its type from the assigned value and locks the variable to that type for the rest of its scope.
let vs const — Widened vs Narrow Types

TypeScript infers differently for let and const because their mutability differs. const variables can never be reassigned, so TypeScript keeps the literal type. let variables can be reassigned, so TypeScript widens the type to the base primitive.
TypeScript infers the return type of a function from its return statements. You rarely need to annotate return types explicitly — but doing so documents intent and catches mistakes earlier.

TypeScript also infers types from context — where a value is used, not just where it's created. This is called contextual typing and applies to callbacks, object literals passed to typed parameters, and template expressions.
Generic functions infer their type arguments from the arguments passed at the call site. You rarely need to write the angle-bracket type explicitly.
The infer keyword lets you capture a type during conditional type evaluation. It is how utility types like ReturnType and Parameters are implemented.
Widening — When Inference Becomes Less Specific

Type widening happens when TypeScript generalises a literal type to its base type. It occurs on let declarations, when a literal is returned from a function without a return annotation, and when values are passed into wider contexts.
After a type guard check, TypeScript narrows the type within the guarded block. The inferred type inside the branch is more specific than the declared type.
String literal not assignable to union param
Omitting return annotation on recursive function

When TypeScript infers the type of an array or a union across branches, it runs the "best common type" algorithm: it examines every candidate type and picks the supertype that all candidates are assignable to. If no candidate is a supertype of the rest, the result is a union of all candidates.
A user-defined type predicate (x is T) teaches TypeScript to narrow a type after a function call. The inferred type inside the if block changes based on the predicate's declared return type.
One of the practical benefits of inference is that it keeps code lean. Over-annotating adds noise without improving safety. Use these rules to decide when to annotate.
Annotate when inference gives the wrong type or when the annotation documents a public contract. Let inference handle everything else.
When Inference Slows You Down: Explicit Beats Clever
TypeScript's inference engine derives types automatically from context, keeping code concise while maintaining type safety:
TypeScript infers from the assigned value;
inferred from all return paths; annotate explicitly for public APIs, complex logic, or recursive functions
TypeScript types callback parameters, event handlers, and object literals from the expected type at the call site
type arguments are inferred from call-site arguments; only provide them explicitly when inference gives
to preserve literal types on objects and arrays returned from functions or stored in
for empty arrays; HTTP generics type the Observable automatically
